# A Framework for AI Opportunities

<!-- 
Main purpose: Practical framework to prompt thinking beyond chatbots
Key insight: Four categories help identify feasible AI applications
-->

## The Default Answer Problem

- Libraries default to "Let's build a chatbot!"
- Chat interfaces rarely the best starting point
- Need framework for identifying practical applications
<!-- Examples of failed chatbot projects -->
<!-- Cost/benefit analysis of chat vs other approaches -->

## Four Ways Libraries Can Use AI

<!-- This framework helps categorize and evaluate opportunities -->

### 1. Transform

**Converting content from one format to another**

- **OCR**: Image → searchable text
- **Transcription**: Audio/video → text  
- **Translation**: Language A → Language B
- **Format conversion**: Unstructured → structured data

<!-- Why it works well: -->
- Clear inputs and outputs
- Measurable success metrics
- Existing benchmarks
- Direct time savings
- Enables downstream applications

<!-- Examples from libraries: -->
<!-- - Newspaper digitization projects -->
<!-- - Oral history transcription -->
<!-- - Multilingual catalog records -->

### 2. Extract & Describe

**Pulling out information or creating summaries**

- **Named entity extraction**: People, places, dates, organizations
- **Summarization**: Abstracts, brief descriptions
- **Metadata generation**: Cataloging information
- **Caption creation**: Visual content description

<!-- Key distinction: -->
<!-- - Extraction = finding existing info -->
<!-- - Description = creating new explanatory content -->

<!-- Use cases: -->
<!-- - Accelerating cataloging workflows -->
<!-- - Making image collections searchable -->
<!-- - Creating finding aids -->

### 3. Organize

**Classification, categorization, and structuring**

- **Subject classification**: Topics, themes
- **Document type identification**: Letters, reports, photos, maps
- **Sensitivity screening**: Public vs restricted
- **Quality assessment**: Conservation needs
- **Language detection**: Routing to catalogers

<!-- The 80% rule: -->
<!-- - Perfect accuracy not required -->
<!-- - Human review for edge cases -->
<!-- - Still massive time savings -->

<!-- Real examples: -->
<!-- - British Library language detection -->
<!-- - National Archives FOI screening -->

### 4. Find

**Discovery, search enhancement, similarity**

- **Semantic search**: Intent beyond keywords
- **Similarity detection**: Related/duplicate items
- **Cross-collection linking**: Finding connections
- **Recommendation systems**: Suggesting content
- **Visual search**: Search by image content

<!-- Technical approaches: -->
<!-- - Embeddings and vector search -->
<!-- - Clustering algorithms -->
<!-- - Graph databases -->

## Using the Framework

### As a Prompting Tool

1. Start with pain points
   <!-- What takes most time? -->
   <!-- What has biggest backlog? -->
   
2. Map to categories
   <!-- Which framework bucket? -->
   <!-- Multiple categories = too complex? -->
   
3. Assess feasibility
   <!-- Do you have training data? -->
   <!-- Clear success criteria? -->
   
4. Evaluate impact
   <!-- Hours saved per week? -->
   <!-- Collections affected? -->

### Identifying Good Candidates

Best opportunities typically:
- Single category focus (not everything)
- Existing examples available
- Frequent occurrence (daily/weekly)
- Imperfection acceptable (80% rule)
- Significant time savings

<!-- Red flags: -->
<!-- - "Do everything" projects -->
<!-- - No existing examples -->
<!-- - Needs 100% accuracy -->
<!-- - One-off tasks -->

### Moving Beyond "Interesting"

<!-- Focus on boring but valuable -->
<!-- Repetitive tasks that consume skilled time -->
<!-- Not the flashy demos -->

## Case Studies

### British Library: Language Detection (Organize)
- Task: ID language of uncatalogued items
- Impact: Route to specialists
- Result: 90% accuracy, 5 hrs/week saved

### National Archives: FOI Screening (Organize)  
- Task: Initial sensitivity assessment
- Impact: Faster response times
- Result: 85% accuracy, 3-day reduction

### State Library: Photo Description (Extract & Describe)
- Task: Describe photograph backlog
- Impact: 50,000 images discoverable
- Result: Weeks vs years

<!-- Add more examples: -->
<!-- - Library of Congress -->
<!-- - European libraries -->
<!-- - University libraries -->

## Workshop Application

Using framework in workshops:

1. Present four categories with examples
2. Departmental brainstorming
3. Map tasks to categories
4. Prioritize by impact and data
5. Select reusable pilots

<!-- Facilitation tips: -->
<!-- - Use physical cards/boards -->
<!-- - Time-box discussions -->
<!-- - Focus on specifics not abstracts -->

<!-- Outcome: -->
<!-- From "we need AI" to "we need AI to classify 500K documents" -->

## Common Patterns

<!-- What emerges from using this framework: -->

- Transform often prerequisite for other categories
- Organize most common pain point
- Extract & Describe highest cataloger value
- Find most user-facing impact

<!-- Combinations that work: -->
<!-- - OCR → Extract → Organize -->
<!-- - Transform → Find -->

## Next Steps

<!-- Transition to workshop chapter -->
<!-- How to run sessions using framework -->
<!-- Gathering requirements effectively -->

---

<!-- 
Additional considerations:
- Technical requirements per category
- Typical accuracy ranges
- Cost implications
- Open source vs commercial tools
- Skills needed for implementation
- How to evaluate vendors using framework
-->

<!-- 
References to add:
- Case study sources
- Framework inspiration (if any)
- Related taxonomies of AI tasks
-->
